text: {"suggestedLabel":"Egg‑Safety Optimist","suggestedDescription":"A perspective that emphasizes moderate egg consumption as generally safe for cardiovascular health, treating risk signals as uncertain or confounded.","stances":[{"claimIndex":0,"stance":"accept","rationale":"A single large egg yolk indeed contains roughly 200 mg of cholesterol, a factual nutritional fact."},{"claimIndex":1,"stance":"accept","rationale":"The evidence shows that dietary cholesterol has only a weak correlation with blood cholesterol levels in most people."},{"claimIndex":2,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Large prospective cohort studies of tens of thousands of men and women found no link between eating up to one egg daily and heart disease."},{"claimIndex":3,"stance":"accept","rationale":"For the average person, the type of dietary fat matters more for blood lipids than the cholesterol found in foods like eggs."},{"claimIndex":4,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"The Physicians’ Health Study suggests a possible risk at very high intakes, but the evidence is not conclusive for typical consumption."},{"claimIndex":5,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"People with difficulty controlling LDL may choose egg whites, but the overall risk from moderate yolk intake remains low."},{"claimIndex":6,"stance":"reject","rationale":"The claim isolates diabetics and ignores the broader evidence that moderate egg intake is not harmful for most people."},{"claimIndex":7,"stance":"reject","rationale":"Limiting eggs to three yolks per week for diabetics is not supported by the bulk of data showing little effect at typical intakes."},{"claimIndex":8,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Compared with sugary, refined‑grain breakfast foods, eggs provide a more favorable nutrient profile for heart health."},{"claimIndex":9,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"Oats with nuts and berries are heart‑healthy, but saying they are \"much better\" than an egg‑centric breakfast overstates the evidence."},{"claimIndex":10,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"Plant proteins are linked to lower mortality, yet the comparison with eggs does not definitively show eggs are inferior."},{"claimIndex":11,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Eggs are a reasonable breakfast option when consumed in moderation, especially alongside plant‑based proteins."},{"claimIndex":12,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Eggs typically remain safe to eat for four to six weeks past the sell‑by date if stored properly."},{"claimIndex":13,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Cooking eggs until both whites and yolks are firm reduces the risk of food‑borne illness."},{"claimIndex":14,"stance":"accept","rationale":"An internal temperature of 160 °F ensures that eggs are safely cooked."},{"claimIndex":15,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Limiting room‑temperature exposure to two hours prevents bacterial growth in cooked eggs."},{"claimIndex":16,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Vegetarian‑fed chickens can develop deficiencies unless their feed is appropriately fortified."},{"claimIndex":17,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Current research does not show that eggs from vegetarian‑fed hens are nutritionally superior to those from standard feeds."},{"claimIndex":18,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Organic eggs are produced by hens that are not caged and have access to outdoor areas."},{"claimIndex":19,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Egg shell color does not affect nutritional content; brown and white eggs are nutritionally equivalent."},{"claimIndex":20,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Hen breed determines egg color and size, producing a range from white to blue‑green shells."},{"claimIndex":21,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Yolk color reflects the hen's diet, not the egg's nutritional value."},{"claimIndex":22,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Egg sizes vary, but large eggs are the standard for most recipes."},{"claimIndex":23,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Egg muffins can be refrigerated for up to five days or frozen for about a month."},{"claimIndex":24,"stance":"accept","rationale":"While chicken eggs dominate the U.S. market, duck and quail eggs are also consumed worldwide."},{"claimIndex":25,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Chicken eggs provide an affordable source of high‑quality protein and other nutrients."},{"claimIndex":26,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Eggs naturally contain a high amount of cholesterol, primarily in the yolk."},{"claimIndex":27,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Cholesterol from eggs does not raise blood cholesterol as dramatically as trans‑fats or saturated fats."},{"claimIndex":28,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"Some studies report a link between egg intake and heart disease, but the overall evidence remains mixed."},{"claimIndex":29,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Accompanying foods like bacon and sausage contribute more to cardiovascular risk than the eggs themselves."},{"claimIndex":30,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Cooking methods, especially frying in added fats, are more influential on heart‑disease risk than egg consumption per se."},{"claimIndex":31,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Most healthy adults can safely eat up to seven eggs per week without increasing heart‑disease risk."},{"claimIndex":32,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Evidence suggests that consuming up to seven eggs weekly may lower the risk of certain stroke subtypes."},{"claimIndex":33,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Egg intake at moderate levels has been associated with a reduced risk of age‑related macular degeneration."},{"claimIndex":34,"stance":"reject","rationale":"The claim that seven eggs a week raise heart‑disease risk in diabetics conflicts with the broader data showing minimal effect at typical intakes."},{"claimIndex":35,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Other research finds no connection between seven weekly eggs and heart disease in diabetics, supporting a neutral stance."},{"claimIndex":36,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"Some investigations hint at a possible link between egg consumption and diabetes onset, but the evidence is not definitive."},{"claimIndex":37,"stance":"reject","rationale":"Current guidelines no longer emphasize minimizing dietary cholesterol to 300 mg; the focus is on overall dietary pattern."},{"claimIndex":38,"stance":"accept","rationale":"A large egg provides about 186 mg of cholesterol, all located in the yolk."},{"claimIndex":39,"stance":"accept","rationale":"When other dietary cholesterol sources are low, an egg a day is generally considered acceptable by many studies."},{"claimIndex":40,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Egg whites contain no cholesterol and are a good source of protein."},{"claimIndex":41,"stance":"accept","rationale":"The Physicians Committee for Responsible Medicine performed a comprehensive review of egg‑cholesterol studies up to March 2019."},{"claimIndex":42,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Industry funding of egg research has risen from 0 % in the 1950s to about 60 % in the 2010‑2019 decade."},{"claimIndex":43,"stance":"reject","rationale":"The claim that over 85 % of studies show unfavorable effects ignores the large body of neutral or favorable findings."},{"claimIndex":44,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Industry‑funded trials are more likely to report conclusions that differ from their actual results compared with non‑industry studies."},{"claimIndex":45,"stance":"accept","rationale":"A non‑significant cholesterol change indicates at least a 5 % chance the result is due to random variation."},{"claimIndex":46,"stance":"accept","rationale":"The 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee concluded that dietary cholesterol has no appreciable effect on serum cholesterol."},{"claimIndex":47,"stance":"uncertain","rationale":"A 2019 meta‑analysis reported a modest LDL increase of about nine points per egg, but the clinical relevance is debated."},{"claimIndex":48,"stance":"reject","rationale":"Stating that 139 of 153 studies show eggs raise cholesterol overstates the consensus; many studies show no effect."},{"claimIndex":49,"stance":"reject","rationale":"Only a subset of those studies reached statistical significance, and the overall impact remains small."},{"claimIndex":50,"stance":"accept","rationale":"No studies have documented a significant net decrease in cholesterol from egg consumption."},{"claimIndex":51,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Six non‑industry studies observed non‑significant trends toward lower cholesterol, but these findings are not conclusive."},{"claimIndex":52,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Eight industry‑funded studies also reported non‑significant cholesterol decreases, underscoring the mixed results."},{"claimIndex":53,"stance":"accept","rationale":"The Physicians Committee for Responsible Medicine was founded in 1985 as a nonprofit organization."},{"claimIndex":54,"stance":"accept","rationale":"For most people, eating an egg a day does not increase the risk of heart attack, stroke, or other cardiovascular events."},{"claimIndex":55,"stance":"accept","rationale":"A large egg supplies roughly 6 g of protein and 72 kcal."},{"claimIndex":56,"stance":"accept","rationale":"A large egg provides about 270 IU of vitamin A and 41 IU of vitamin D."},{"claimIndex":57,"stance":"accept","rationale":"One large egg delivers approximately 6 g of protein and 72 calories."},{"claimIndex":58,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Large prospective cohort studies following hundreds of thousands over decades found no higher cardiovascular event rates among those eating up to one egg daily."},{"claimIndex":59,"stance":"accept","rationale":"Highly refined carbohydrates in foods like white toast and pastries are linked to increased cardiovascular risk."},{"claimIndex":60,"stance":"accept","rationale":"About one‑third of American adults have hypertension, highlighting the importance of overall dietary patterns."}]}
